question: A 60-year-old man underwent CT evaluation of an upper abdominal process and subsequently required percutaneous catheter drainage. Based on the imaging, which of the following characteristics in this patient's scan most strongly predicts biliary complications such as persistent biliary drainage?

options.A: Large, centrally located hepatic collection (>5 cm)
options.D: Peripheral capsular hepatic lesion abutting diaphragm

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the axial CT image for size and location of the hepatic collection. The scan shows a single, large, well‑circumscribed hypodense collection within the central portion of the liver (left of midline in this slice), clearly exceeding a few centimeters in diameter and located adjacent to the porta hepatis/central hepatic hilum.

Next, I relate those imaging findings to the question, which asks which scan characteristic most strongly predicts biliary complications. The available answer choices describe distinct patterns by size and location (large central >5 cm; small subcapsular <5 cm; multiple small intrahepatic; peripheral capsular abutting diaphragm).

Then, I consider how each option lines up with what I see: the image does not show a small subcapsular collection or multiple small collections, nor a peripheral capsular lesion abutting the diaphragm. Instead, the image clearly demonstrates a single, large, centrally located hepatic collection that would be categorized as >5 cm and central.

Finally, tying the observations to the choices, the scan appearance most closely corresponds to option A (large, centrally located hepatic collection >5 cm), so that is the best selection based on the imaged features.
caption: Axial computed tomography of a 60-year-old man showing a large abscess in segment IV of the liver near the porta hepatis. Note the duct dilation (arrows) that resulted from rupture of the abscess into the central bile ducts. He was managed with catheter drainage. Bilious fluid draining through the catheter was observed for several weeks in this patient.
sub_caption: Axial computed tomography of a 60-year-old man showing a large abscess in segment IV of the liver near the porta hepatis. Note the duct dilation (arrows) that resulted from rupture of the abscess into the central bile ducts. He was managed with catheter drainage. Bilious fluid draining through the catheter was observed for several weeks in this patient.
image_context: A common cause of drug failure is the presence of biliary complications, which has been reported to occur in up to 27% of refractory cases[12,22,77]. This occurs either from ductal communication with the abscess or from external compression by a large abscess[12,41]. When the liver parenchyma is destroyed by an aggressive abscess, the bile ducts are also damaged, producing ductal communications[12]. Usually, the communication is subtle, and therefore, ductal dilatation may not be evident on imaging. In several cases, the diagnosis is made only during percutaneous drainage when the initial aspirated fluid is bilious or when bile (usually persistent) appears thereafter[11,22,77]. Uncommonly, an abscess, particularly when large and aggressive, can rupture into the central bile ducts, causing duct dilation (Figure <xref rid="WJR-14-272-g004" ref-type="fig">4</xref>). In such cases, the diagnosis may be confirmed when endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) or cavitogram demonstrates contrast extravasation into the abscess cavity[). In such cases, the diagnosis may be confirmed when endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) or cavitogram demonstrates contrast extravasation into the abscess cavity[22,55]. Usually, the bile ducts are compressed by a large abscess, resulting in biliary duct dilation; these cases are evident on ultrasound or CT. The size and location of an abscess on imaging can provide anatomic clues to the presence of a biliary complication. The large (> 5 to 10 cm) and centrally located abscesses (near porta hepatis) are more likely to have biliary compilations than those smaller and with subcapsular locations[12]. Clinically, the presence of high jaundice may indicate biliary complications. Agarwal et al[22] compared the abscesses with and without biliary communications and found that total bilirubin levels > 2 mg/dL were present only in the patients with biliary complications.
